title: TBM 368: Goals Take Practice
questions: Why do teams struggle with OKRs even when they adopt a shared framework?
What goal-setting challenges do different frameworks (SMART, BHAG, FAST, WOOP, etc.) each address?
How can teams build practice for goal setting more frequently than quarterly OKRs?
What other goal types should teams consider beyond OKRs (inputs, milestones, delivery, launch, north star)?
search_terms: why OKRs don't magically solve goal setting
goal-setting frameworks SMART BHAG FAST WOOP HARD CLEAR GROW PACT
practice goal setting more frequently than quarterly
summary: The post argues that while OKRs improve clarity and review cadence, they cannot eliminate the inherent difficulty of setting and refining goals. It’s aimed at product and team leaders who are seeing OKR struggles and need a mindset shift: goal setting is a continuous practice across many interconnected goal types, not a quarterly exercise.

title: TBM 324: Goal-O-Rama
questions: What types of goals should we use in an OKR system beyond just targets?
How do we write anti-goals (NOKRs) that clarify strategy and expectations?
What makes a milestone meaningful versus just an arbitrary date?
How can we connect leading inputs to lagging outcomes while staying in learning mode?
summary: The post presents a practical taxonomy of goal types (e.g., primary/objective, target, anti-goal, guardrail, leading/lagging, milestones, process, adaptive, decision-based, capability-building) and argues for mixing them to improve clarity and execution. It’s aimed at teams and practitioners using OKRs or similar goal systems who want better goal design, stronger learning loops, and fewer misleading milestones.

title: TBM 309: Zombie Practices and Processes
questions: Why do OKRs fail in large companies even when they work in small teams?
How does a practice drift from its original purpose into a zombie process?
What incentives and organizational dynamics cause OKRs to become performance-management paperwork?
What should leaders do to fight mission drift and restore usefulness to teams?
summary: This post explains why OKRs (and similar management practices) often degrade into “zombie processes” in large organizations, despite being straightforward in theory. It is aimed at product, engineering, and leadership teams trying to restore strategic focus and alignment by addressing mission drift, incentive misalignment, and the behavioral conditions that make practices stick or fail.

title: TBM 305: Stop the (Goal) Cascade Madness
questions: Why do goal cascades and multi-layer strategy cascades become optics-driven box-checking?
How should front-line teams structure stable core metrics versus temporary bet metrics?
What signals indicate a team is “works on whatever” and lacks stable domain alignment?
How can leadership shift strategy without forcing teams to reinvent metrics every quarter?
search_terms: stable core metrics and bet metrics
summary: This post argues that goal cascades are frequently ineffective because they confuse hierarchical org charts with causal metric models. It provides a metrics-first approach for front-line teams: maintain stable core metrics over long horizons while using temporary bet metrics to test local initiatives and connect them to core outcomes. The guidance is aimed at leaders and product/engineering organizations trying to improve quarterly goal-setting and strategy execution without building artificial multi-level cascades.

title: TBM 225: Milestones and Stepping Stones
questions: How can we use milestones to create real integration and learning rather than hollow checklists?
What makes a milestone a forcing function that teams can’t ignore?
How do we design milestones that feel appropriately uncomfortable and end-to-end?
Should we think of milestones as linear mile markers or as stepping stones with options?
search_terms: scope cut not work harder milestone
summary: The post argues that the language of “milestones” can be used to improve how teams execute product work by creating forcing-function goals that drive integration, urgency, and learning. It’s aimed at product and delivery leaders who feel stuck between delivery-centric checklists and distant outcome-centric planning, and want a practical way to design near-term milestones (or “stepping stones”) that preserve options while moving “from here to there.”

title: TBM 216: Good Goals/Bad Goals
questions: How can I tell whether a goal is good or bad?
What makes a goal enabling versus constraining in harmful ways?
How do we prevent zombie goals and keep goals updated with new information?
Should we prioritize meaningful impact over perfect measurement when setting goals?
summary: The post argues that effective goal setting goes beyond SMART/OKRs by treating good goals as enabling constraints with guardrails, context, adaptability, and value alignment. It is aimed at individuals and teams who want to improve decision-making and execution by identifying and avoiding “bad goals” that create rigidity, distraction, performative behavior, or even exploitation. It also encourages readers to evaluate what behaviors goals actually drive and to iterate goal-setting practices over time.

title: TBM 213: Goal Cascades vs. High-Conviction Models
questions: How can we tell whether our OKR goal cascade is creating high conviction or just wishful thinking?
What’s the difference between transactional OKRs and long-term OKRs that require a high-conviction model?
How do we build a high-conviction model that links actionable inputs to sustainable outcomes?
What is the “irrational-looking” but high-conviction practice at our company that outsiders might not understand?
search_terms: goal cascades vs high conviction models
how to build conviction in planning
summary: The post argues that many OKR goal cascades fail because they generate low conviction when they link non-actionable goals to uncertain long-term outcomes. It’s aimed at product and leadership teams trying to improve planning, alignment, and decision quality by using high-conviction models (often supported by a north star metric) to connect actionable inputs to sustainable growth.

title: TBM 42/52: Fluffy & Ambiguous
questions: How do I write objectives that leave room for creativity without becoming fluffy and ambiguous?
Why do my PMs seem to agree but produce roadmaps that feel scattered?
What should I do when newer PMs lack the implicit context that made earlier alignment easy?
How can I tell whether an objective is specific enough to inspire coherent actions and good questions?
search_terms: onboarding PMs with context and clarity
summary: This post helps product leaders and PM orgs craft objectives and guiding principles that preserve creativity while avoiding ambiguity that causes misaligned roadmaps. It explains why newer PMs may respond with ambivalence when they lack the implicit context that more tenured PMs rely on, and offers practical tests for whether objectives are specific, opinionated, and question-inviting. Intended for product leaders, product managers, and teams using OKR-style planning who want clearer strategy without over-prescription.

title: TBM 41/51: Why Goal Cascades are Harmful (and What to Do Instead)
questions: Why do goal cascades lead to misalignment and reduced congruence across teams?
How can we set goals without tying them to the org chart hierarchy?
What should goals be based on: hierarchical priorities or driver models and leverage points?
How do we distinguish inputs, outputs, and measurement when designing a goal system?
search_terms: network of goals instead of hierarchy
summary: The post argues that hierarchical goal cascades (few top-level goals replicated fractally) often create misalignment by reinforcing org boundaries, oversimplifying variable relationships, and conflating inputs/outputs with structure. It recommends an alternative for strategy and planning: build an org-agnostic driver model with measurement, identify leverage points, and then set goals based on the model (potentially with multiple layers and time horizons). The guidance is aimed at leaders and teams designing goal-setting and operating mechanisms for organizational congruence.

title: TBM 24/52: Beware of the Cascade
questions: How can I tell when our goals and work are turning into a damaging cascade?
What makes the North Star Framework different from a traditional goal cascade?
How do I redesign goal/metric relationships to improve flexibility and customer relevance?
What practical steps can we take to flatten layers and reduce input-focused, time-bound work?
search_terms: goal decomposition vs north star framework
input focused vs output focused goals
summary: This post explains how hierarchical goal/work cascades can degrade flexibility, creativity, and customer relevance by pushing teams toward prescriptive, input-focused, time-bound execution. It contrasts cascading structures with the North Star Framework, arguing that metrics can guide work without a top-down strategy-to-project hierarchy. It offers practical ways to recognize and fix cascade dynamics, including flattening levels and reframing relationships as leading indicators.

title: TBM 16/52: Goal Clarity (and Embracing Different Perspectives)
questions: Why do teams stay misaligned even when everyone agrees on the same goal statement?
How can we make goals clearer without over-specifying solutions?
What conversations should we have to surface assumptions and out-of-scope boundaries in a goal?
How do we handle different experience levels when interpreting and acting on goals?
search_terms: interpretation gaps in OKRs or quarterly goals
why goals fail to align teams
summary: The post argues that even a well-written quarterly goal will be interpreted differently by team members, leading to misalignment. It is aimed at managers and team leads who want practical ways to improve goal clarity through shared understanding, explicit conversations about interpretations and decision impact, and psychological safety to iterate on goals. It also emphasizes respecting different experience levels so that newer or less confident contributors can engage meaningfully.

title: TBM 11/52: Healthy Goals
questions: How can you tell whether your goal-setting framework is helping or harming your team?
What signals show that goals are supporting psychological safety and real accountability?
How do you design goals to encourage sustainability, focus, and aligned autonomy?
What should you do when team members can’t admit they need help or that goals were too hard?
search_terms: how to make goals work for you
summary: The post argues that goal-setting frameworks only work when they create a safe, motivating environment that supports sustainability, focus, and aligned autonomy. It’s aimed at managers and team leads who want to use goals for learning and progress rather than for blame, crunch-time, or restricting collaboration. The key test is whether team members can speak honestly when they miss goals and ask for help, tools, or process changes.

title: TBM 25/53: Persistent Models vs. Point-In-Time Goals
questions: How do you balance persistent models (strategy-level) with point-in-time goals (quarterly work) in your organization?
Why do quarterly OKRs feel like a “big deal” for some teams, and how does the absence of persistent models contribute?
What should teams build first: a persistent model or initiative/OKR goal setting?
How can teams use a north star metric and causal inputs to inform OKRs without implying a specific end date?
summary: The post contrasts persistent models (e.g., north star metrics and causal inputs that endure for 1–3 years) with point-in-time goals (e.g., quarterly OKRs tied to a specific time span). It argues that teams struggle and overcomplicate quarterly goal setting when they lack persistent models, and that coaching should emphasize building persistent models before setting initiative goals. It is aimed at product and strategy leaders who want better alignment between long-term value creation and short-term execution.

title: TBM 2/53: Three Options
questions: What options did we consider but reject, and why?
How can we frame the problem so the team can generate at least three distinct, credible paths to the goal?
Does our OKR objective unintentionally eliminate alternative options (e.g., “deliver X” with no other viable approach)?
When should we revisit the selected strategy, and what evidence would change our confidence?
summary: This post argues that product and strategy conversations move faster and build more trust when teams explicitly surface three options they considered, including the ones they rejected and the reasoning behind that choice. It’s aimed at product managers, designers, and coaches who want better decision-making hygiene, clearer context for new team members, and stronger goal-setting (including OKR quality) through forcing alternative paths to be considered.
